chapterTitle: The Lighthouse That Hasn't Been Built Yet
imageCaption: A lighthouse made of pure light—structure and beacon indistinguishable—stands where coordinates say Curaçao should be but the island hasn't arrived yet. Sully stands at the bow, barking at something only he can see.
text:
Sully started barking three minutes before anyone could see anything.

It was the one-long-bark kind, the serious kind, which meant something major was happening. Aaliyah was in the navigation cabin reviewing Lulu's journal for the hundredth time—looking for patterns in the coffee-shop discoveries and the timing of spontaneous decisions—when she heard the alert from the deck. She didn't think. She just moved, which by now was becoming her default setting: something happens, Aaliyah runs toward it instead of away from it.

Her entire family was already at the bow.

"What are we looking at?" Aaliyah asked, positioning herself next to her father, who was holding the pegboard of license plates with the intensity of someone who'd learned that his souvenir collection had apparently become navigational equipment.

"Something that shouldn't be there," River said. The AI's voice was coming through every speaker on the boat simultaneously, which meant it was important enough to interrupt the usual hierarchy of communication. "Three nautical miles out, bearing one-seven-zero. Visual confirmation is happening in approximately forty-five seconds."

"Can I get a less-technical answer?" Elijah asked hopefully. He was holding Sully, which had taken considerable effort because Sully was genuinely alarmed and wanted to be free to investigate, but Elijah was learning that sometimes keeping a dog contained was an act of love.

Then Aaliyah saw it.

A lighthouse. Except not a lighthouse. It was lighthouse-shaped but made entirely of light—not illuminated light, but light that existed as structure, as material, as the actual physical thing the beam was supposed to be made of. It was rotating, but not the way lighthouses rotate. This one was folding light back in on itself, creating this impossible architecture where the beam became the building became the beam.

And it was pointing directly at them.

"Oh," Luisana said softly, her journalist instincts clearly trying to categorize this into something that made sense. "Oh, that's—"

"Impossible," Aaliyah finished. "Lighthouses don't rotate inward. Light doesn't form structure. This isn't—"

"Navigation," River interrupted gently. "This is the glitch that answers the question you've been asking since the broadcast bleed. The question about who was supposed to meet you."

Anthony had gone very still. His hand, holding the pegboard of license plates, had started to tremble slightly. Aaliyah watched him, trying to understand what he was seeing that she wasn't.

"Lulu," he said quietly. "Do you see the door?"

There was a door. How had Aaliyah not noticed the door? But now that Anthony had mentioned it, she could see it—small, yellow, impossibly present in the structure of a light-made lighthouse that shouldn't exist. It was exactly the color of every image Luisana had ever bookmarked on a Curaçao real estate site, as if the island itself had been consulting her browser history.

"River," Aaliyah said, understanding beginning to crystallize around her question, "is that the island?"

"No," River said. "That's the lighthouse that the island has been building for you across all the timelines. The one place where all your possible routes converge. The threshold."

Elijah had finally gotten free of Sully, who immediately bolted to the railing and started barking at the lighthouse like it owed him money. "Is the island on the other side?" he asked, with the matter-of-fact clarity that only a nine-year-old who'd accepted temporal glitches as normal could muster.

"The island exists on the other side of every threshold you've created together," River replied. "Every choice, every spontaneous decision, every moment you've chosen chaos over optimization—those choices built that lighthouse. It's your family's resonance frequency made visible. It's a door made of every decision you've ever made when nobody was planning it."

Luisana had her journal out. Her pen was moving, but Aaliyah could tell her mother wasn't actually writing—her hand was shaking too much for words. This was that thing Luisana did when she encountered something so true it bypassed language entirely and went straight to viscera.

"How do we get through?" Anthony asked.

"You already have," River said. "Seventeen times. You're asking the question that means you're ready to walk through it. That's the real passage. The question itself is the threshold."

The lighthouse was closer now. Or maybe the boat was moving toward it. Or maybe distance had stopped meaning anything in this particular pocket of spacetime, and proximity was just a matter of decision. Sully had stopped barking and instead was doing that thing where he pressed his entire body against the railing, vibrating with the intensity of a dog who'd just spotted something he'd been waiting for his entire life.

Aaliyah understood that feeling.

She pulled out her phone, the one that kept not-existing during glitches but which was being remarkably persistent today. She took a photograph of the lighthouse—not for documentation this time, but because some moments needed to be held in a specific format. Proof. Memory. Threshold.

"Tomorrow," River said, "you arrive."

"Tomorrow?" Elijah sounded outraged. "But I want to go through the door right now."

"The lighthouse exists between today and tomorrow," River explained. "It's the moment of transition. You have to spend one more night knowing where you're going but not being there yet. That's the final thing a spontaneous family has to learn: that sometimes waiting is what makes arrival matter."

Anthony put his arm around Luisana, who had given up on writing in her journal and was simply staring at the yellow door with the intensity of someone who'd been dreaming about it and now had to reconcile the dream with the reality of its actual existence.

"One more night," Luisana repeated, like she was testing the words, seeing if they could hold all the meaning they suddenly needed to carry.

"One more night," her husband echoed, and Aaliyah could hear in his voice the wonder of a man who'd spent his whole life believing in spontaneous moments and was about to discover that sometimes spontaneity led somewhere permanent.

Sully started barking again, but this time it wasn't an alarm. It was an announcement. It was a celebration. It was a dog saying to anyone who would listen: tomorrow you're going home, and I knew it all along.

The lighthouse folded back into pure light as the sun began to set, and the boat sailed into evening with a yellow door waiting just barely beyond the horizon.
